symbols.name: \nu
symbols.def: Common dominating measure for the full-data classes.

symbols.name: O_i
symbols.def: Observed unit \(i\), with treatment and outcome components \(A_i\) and \(Y_i\).

symbols.name: \mu
symbols.def: Common dominating measure for observed-data laws.

symbols.name: e_P
symbols.def: A fixed Borel version of \(P(A=1\mid X=\cdot)\).

symbols.name: m_{P,a}
symbols.def: A fixed Borel version of \(E_P[Y\mid A=a,X=\cdot]\), for \(a\in\{0,1\}\).

symbols.name: s_0
symbols.def: Hölder smoothness of the exact-tie stratum.

symbols.name: L_0
symbols.def: Hölder radius of the exact-tie stratum.

symbols.name: s_1
symbols.def: Hölder smoothness of the near-tie stratum.

symbols.name: L_1
symbols.def: Hölder radius of the near-tie stratum.

symbols.name: \alpha
symbols.def: Margin exponent of the near-tie stratum.

symbols.name: C
symbols.def: Margin constant of the near-tie stratum.

symbols.name: \beta
symbols.def: Fixed boundary power used in the sparse near-tie bump.

symbols.name: C_n
symbols.def: A sample-measurable, almost surely finite connected closed interval \(C_n=[L_n,U_n]\) with \(L_n\le U_n\).

symbols.name: \psi
symbols.def: Boundary-vanishing reference bump on \( [0,1]^d\), extended by zero outside that cube.

symbols.name: h_n
symbols.def: \(h_n=c_h n^{-2/D}\), where \(c_h>0\) is a fixed sufficiently small constant.

symbols.name: \mathcal C_n
symbols.def: The lexicographically first \(N_n\) pairwise disjoint cubes of side \(h_n\) lying in \( [1/4,3/4]^d\).

symbols.name: c_{\ell,n}
symbols.def: Lower-left corner of the \(\ell\)-th cube in \(\mathcal C_n\).

symbols.name: \theta
symbols.def: Rademacher sign vector indexing a sparse near-tie law.

symbols.name: \Delta_{\theta,n}
symbols.def: Sparse signed contrast on the cubes \(\mathcal C_n\).

symbols.name: P_0^\circ
symbols.def: Observed image of the zero-mean randomized Rademacher full-data law in \(\mathrm{def:base-law}\).

symbols.name: K
symbols.def: Finite constant in the sparse-mixture chi-square bound.

symbols.name: c_{\gamma,\varepsilon}
symbols.def: Positive constant depending only on \(\gamma\) and \(\varepsilon\).

symbols.name: C_n^\dagger
symbols.def: Connected interval sequence associated with the union-calibrated route.

novel.justification: An exact-treatment-response tie stratum describes settings in which either treatment has the same conditional mean for every baseline profile; it is a law-level benchmark for the exceptional boundary.

standard.name: Tsybakov margin condition with an allowed tie atom

definitions.name: admissible ordered pair
definitions.construction: Write \(s=r+\tau\) with \(r=\lfloor s\rfloor\) and \(\tau\in[0,1)\); \(C^s(\mathcal X)\) has bounded continuous derivatives through order \(r\), with order-\(r\) derivatives \(\tau\)-Hölder when \(\tau>0\). Fix \(0<s_1<s_0\), \(L_0,L_1>0\), and \(\alpha,C>0\) satisfying \(s_1\alpha<1\), and choose \(\beta\in(s_1,1/\alpha)\).

definitions.name: exact-tie base law
definitions.construction: Let \(X\sim\operatorname{Unif}(\mathcal X)\), let \(A\sim\operatorname{Bernoulli}(1/2)\) independently, and conditionally independently draw \(Y(0),Y(1)\in\{-B,B\}\) with conditional means zero.  Let \(P_0^\circ\) be its observed image.

definitions.name: sparse randomized-sign near-tie family
definitions.construction: For each sufficiently large \(n\), use \(\mathcal C_n\) and \(\Delta_{\theta,n}\). Let \(X\sim\operatorname{Unif}(\mathcal X)\), let \(A\sim\operatorname{Bernoulli}(1/2)\) independently, set \(E_F[Y(1)\mid X=x]=\Delta_{\theta,n}(x)/2\) and \(E_F[Y(0)\mid X=x]=-\Delta_{\theta,n}(x)/2\), and conditionally independently draw \(Y(a)\in\{-B,B\}\) with the corresponding Rademacher probabilities.  The constants \(c_h,c_a,c_N\) are fixed small enough that every displayed mean lies in \([-\bar B,\bar B]\), every conditional variance is at least \(v_0\), and the Hölder and margin bounds hold.

definitions.name: sequence-level honest adaptation criterion
definitions.construction: For every positive deterministic \(a=(a_n)_n\), use \(\mathfrak C^{\mathrm{asy}}(\mathcal U,\gamma)\) and \(\mathfrak R^{\mathrm{asy}}_j(a)\) exactly as defined in the symbol table; honesty is a property of the entire interval sequence.

definitions.name: union-calibrated upper-construction handle
definitions.construction: Construct a connected interval by fitting multiscale contrast estimates on training folds, using independent holdout folds to form simultaneous confidence bounds for their dyadic block coefficients, and calibrating the final interval to the maximum smoothing and nuisance bias over both \(\mathcal F_0\) and \(\mathcal F_1\).

statements.statement: Under \(\mathrm{ass:consistency}\), \(\mathrm{ass:exchangeability}\), \(\mathrm{ass:randomization}\), \(\mathrm{ass:bounded-outcomes}\), and \(\mathrm{ass:variance-floor}\), consistency, conditional exchangeability, and positivity from known randomization identify \(m_{P,a}(x)=E_F[Y(a)\mid X=x]\) for \(P_X\)-almost every \(x\) and each \(a\in\{0,1\}\); consequently \(|m_{P,a}(x)|\le\bar B=\sqrt{B^2-v_0}\) for \(P_X\)-almost every \(x\).
statements.justification: Identification precedes the conditional second-moment calculation that yields the envelope.
statements.gap: Both identification and the envelope are standard supporting facts, not contributions.
statements.consumer: It supplies the outcome slack used by the sparse family.

statements.statement: The base law in \(\mathrm{def:base-law}\) belongs to \(\mathcal F_0\).  For every admissible pair, the zero-contrast law also belongs to \(\mathcal F_1\); hence both observed-data strata and \(\mathcal U\) are nonempty.
statements.justification: A union-honesty problem is meaningful only for compatible dominated full-data classes.
statements.gap: This is a model audit rather than a claimed advance over optimal-value procedures.
statements.consumer: It validates the ordered pair used in the lower-bound calculation.

statements.statement: For every \(P\in\mathcal P_0\), \(E_P[\widehat V_{0,n}]=V(P)\), and \(P^n\{V(P)\in C_{0,n}^{\mathrm{or}}\}\ge1-\gamma\).  Consequently \(\sup_{P\in\mathcal P_0}E_{P^n}[\operatorname{length}(C_{0,n}^{\mathrm{or}})]=O(n^{-1/2})\).
statements.justification: When the contrast is identically zero, the value is an ordinary randomized-arm mean.
statements.gap: This is the oracle benchmark, not a novelty claim; Luedtke--van der Laan already motivate regular versus exceptional optimal-value inference.
statements.consumer: It defines the root-\(n\) precision that union honesty may fail to retain at the exact-tie stratum.

statements.statement: For all sufficiently large \(n\) and every \(\theta\in\{-1,1\}^{N_n}\), \(F_{\theta,n}\in\mathcal F_1\), \(P_{\theta,n}\in\mathcal P_1\), and \(V(P_{\theta,n})-V(P_0^\circ)=\delta_n\).  Moreover \(\delta_n\asymp n^{-\xi}\).
statements.justification: The signed construction keeps the causal max functional positive while the signs conceal the alternatives from product testing.
statements.gap: Audibert--Tsybakov give classification-margin constructions, but not this full-data randomized causal max-functional family with an exact-tie oracle stratum.
statements.consumer: It supplies the explicit cross-stratum alternatives for the adaptation lower bound.

statements.statement: For every admissible pair, there are fixed admissible choices of \(c_h,c_a,c_N\) and \(\varepsilon\in(0,1-2\gamma)\) such that \(\chi^2(\overline Q_n,(P_0^\circ)^n)\le\exp\{K n^2N_na_n^4h_n^{2d}\}-1\le\varepsilon^2\) eventually. Hence every \((C_n)_n\in\mathfrak C^{\mathrm{asy}}(\mathcal U,\gamma)\) obeys \(\liminf_{n\to\infty}E_{(P_0^\circ)^n}[\operatorname{length}(C_n)]/\delta_n\ge c_{\gamma,\varepsilon}>0\). If, in addition, \(3s_1\alpha<d\), then \(\xi<1/2\), so union honesty forces a strictly slower-than-root-\(n\) expected length at an exact-tie law even though \(\mathcal P_0\) alone admits \(O(n^{-1/2})\) length.
statements.justification: The sparse-mixture chi-square and expected-length conclusions hold for every admissible pair; \(3s_1\alpha<d\) is reserved solely for the strict slower-than-root-\(n\) comparison.
statements.gap: The explicit causal mixture establishes one component of the lower frontier but does not by itself include the parametric root-\(n\) obstruction.
statements.consumer: Together with the parametric tie lower bound, it determines the unavoidable scale faced by a union-honest clinical interval at the exact-tie law.

statements.statement: Does the union-calibrated route in \(\mathrm{def:union-upper-route}\) yield a sequence \((C_n^\dagger)_n\in\mathfrak C^{\mathrm{asy}}(\mathcal U,\gamma)\) with \(\sup_{P\in\mathcal P_0}E_{P^n}[\operatorname{length}(C_n^\dagger)]=O(\delta_n)\) and the corresponding near-tie risk controlled on \(\mathcal P_1\)?
statements.justification: The proved symbol \(C_n^\dagger\) denotes the split-sample interval. The separate symbol \(C_n^{\mathrm{ms}}\) denotes the hypothetical multiscale route whose sharper rate remains open.
statements.gap: Attainment of \(O(\delta_n)\) by \(C_n^{\mathrm{ms}}\) remains open where it is not excluded by the parametric tie lower bound.
statements.consumer: A sharper answer would determine whether multiscale calibration can improve on the current conservative split-sample clinical interval.

statements.statement: Since \(\xi-1/2=(3s_1\alpha-d)/[2(d+s_1\alpha+4s_1)]\), the displayed sparse family has \(\delta_n\asymp n^{-\xi}\), equal to root-\(n\) order when \(3s_1\alpha=d\) and \(o(n^{-1/2})\) when \(3s_1\alpha>d\). Is a root-\(n\) union-honest connected interval attainable when \(3s_1\alpha\ge d\), or does a different least-favorable family establish a binding obstruction there? When \(3s_1\alpha<d\), can a union-honest interval attain the proved \(n^{-\xi}\) lower scale?
statements.justification: The root-\(n\) subregime \(2s_1\alpha\ge d\) is settled by matching lower and upper scales, while the remaining regimes have an explicit honest bracket.
statements.gap: For \(2s_1\alpha<d\), neither the split-sample upper construction nor the two current lower experiments closes the exponent gap.
statements.consumer: The open regimes specify where a new interval construction or a new least-favorable causal family would change practical precision guarantees.

sampling_model.overlap: The propensity is fixed at \(1/2\) almost everywhere.
sampling_model.nuisance_estimation: The lower-bound family is fully specified and uses no fitted nuisance; the upper construction is an open multiscale sample-splitting route.

bibliography.citation: A. R. Luedtke and M. J. van der Laan (2016). Statistical inference for the mean outcome under a possibly non-unique optimal treatment strategy. \textit{Annals of Statistics} 44, 713--742. doi:10.1214/15-AOS1384.
P. Wu and L. Wang (2021). Inference for optimal treatment regimes. \textit{Biometrics} 77, 465--476. doi:10.1111/biom.13337.

C. Shi, W. Lu, and R. Song (2020). Breaking the curse of nonregularity with subagging: Inference of the mean outcome under optimal treatment regimes. \textit{Journal of Machine Learning Research} 21(176), 1--67.
S. Xu and X. Guo (2026). Optimal inference of the mean outcome under optimal treatment regime. \textit{arXiv:2509.09773}.
J. Whitehouse, Q. Chen, M. Austern, and V. Syrgkanis (2025). Inference on optimal policy values and other irregular functionals via softmax smoothing. \textit{arXiv:2507.11780}.
K. Ponomarev and V. Semenova (2024). Inference on optimal welfare. \textit{arXiv:2410.07443}, revised September 2025.
R. Liu, Z. Liu, Y. Sasaki, and Y. Wan (2025). Nonparametric classification and policy value inference. \textit{arXiv:2511.14700}.
T. T. Cai and M. G. Low (2004). An adaptation theory for nonparametric confidence intervals. \textit{Annals of Statistics} 32, 1805--1840. doi:10.1214/009053604000000049.
T. B. Armstrong and M. Kolesar (2020). Simple and honest confidence intervals in nonparametric regression. \textit{Quantitative Economics} 11, 1--39. doi:10.3982/QE1199.
J.-Y. Audibert and A. B. Tsybakov (2007). Fast learning rates for plug-in classifiers. \textit{Annals of Statistics} 35, 608--633. doi:10.1214/009053606000001217.

W. Liu, Z. Zhang, L. Nie, and G. Soon (2017). A case study in personalized medicine: Rilpivirine versus efavirenz for treatment-naive HIV patients. \textit{Journal of the American Statistical Association} 112, 1381--1392. doi:10.1080/01621459.2017.1280404.

comparator_promise_table.comparator_claim: Proposition 1 gives a standard expected-confidence-length lower-bound reduction from testing pairs for intervals honest over a union.
The article constructs bias-aware honest intervals under specified smoothness restrictions and emphasizes limits on honest data-driven adaptation.
The article characterizes exceptional-law nonregularity and develops optimal-value inference under sufficient conditions.
The article develops nominal-coverage subagging intervals for nonregular optimal values and compares their average lengths.
The article gives smoothed robust and resampling-based inference for an optimal-regime parameter and value.
The preprint develops adaptive smoothing and an asymptotic-variance bound within a robust asymptotically linear unbiased estimator class.
The preprint develops constant-fit softmax-smoothed optimal-policy-value inference and reports efficiency when treatment nonresponse is zero.

The preprint constructs uniformly valid one-sided lower confidence bands robust to non-unique policies through moment-inequality tests and separation or margin considerations, but not the present two-sided connected interval, union-honest expected-length lower bound, or sparse-mixture construction.

The preprint proves Gaussian optimal-policy-value inference using a strictly convex surrogate under distinct representative-policy assumptions, but not the exact-tie ordered union or its two-sided union-honest expected-length lower bound.

tldr: Under iid sampling, known one-half randomization, bounded outcomes, Hölder outcome regressions, and a margin condition that permits a tie atom, a sparse randomized-sign experiment forces every union-honest connected interval for the optimal policy value to have expected length at least \(n^{-1/2}\vee n^{-\xi}\) at the exact-tie base law, where \(\xi=2s_1(\alpha+1)/(d+s_1\alpha+4s_1)\). The explicit split-sample learned-policy interval \(C_n^\dagger\) is uniformly honest and has length \(O(n^{-1/2}+n^{-\rho})\), where \(\rho=s_1(\alpha+1)/(2s_1+d)\). These scales coincide at root \(n\) when \(2s_1\alpha\ge d\); outside that subregime, attainment of the sparse lower scale by the distinct hypothetical multiscale interval \(C_n^{\mathrm{ms}}\) remains open.

project_justification.gap: Existing inference for exceptional optimal-treatment laws does not provide this two-stratum expected-length adaptation diagram for a connected interval that is honest simultaneously over exact ties and Hölder near ties with an allowed tie atom. The unresolved issue is sharply localized: when \(2s_1\alpha<d\), the proved lower exponent \(\min\{1/2,\xi\}\) and achievable exponent \(\min\{1/2,\rho\}\) differ.
project_justification.niche: The note studies honest adaptation for the causal optimal-policy value, taking its randomized-trial identifying functional as given. Its distinctive ingredients are an explicit dominated sparse causal mixture for the lower bound and a single data-driven split-sample learned-policy interval for the upper bound.
project_justification.fill: The lower calculation holds for every admissible pair; the inequality \(3s_1\alpha<d\) is needed only for the corollary that the sparse scale is strictly slower than root \(n\). Combining that calculation with a parametric tie submodel gives the unified lower scale \(n^{-1/2}\vee n^{-\xi}\) on both strata. The proved split-sample interval \(C_n^\dagger\) supplies the honest upper scale \(n^{-1/2}+n^{-\rho}\); the symbol \(C_n^{\mathrm{ms}}\) is reserved for the unresolved multiscale route.

related_work: Luedtke--van der Laan and subsequent work analyze inference at exceptional laws, while policy-learning results supply regret control under smoothness and margin conditions. Cai--Low-style testing arguments explain how indistinguishable alternatives constrain expected interval length, and Armstrong--Kolesar emphasize the cost of honest bias control under predeclared restrictions. Ponomarev--Semenova and Liu--Liu--Sasaki--Wan address related non-uniqueness-robust or representative-policy inference problems. None of those results supplies the present explicit sparse randomized-sign causal product experiment together with this exact-tie/near-tie union bracket. The current result is a proved lower/upper bracket, not a claim of a globally matched frontier.
interpretation: At an exact biomarker-response tie, an analysis restricted to the tie stratum can use a root-\(n\) interval, but a procedure that must also cover nearby heterogeneous treatment effects can be forced to be wider. For individualized rilpivirine-versus-efavirenz regime analysis, the result separates three facts: the unavoidable lower cost, the usable uniformly honest split-sample interval \(C_n^\dagger\), and the remaining mathematical gap represented by the hypothetical multiscale interval \(C_n^{\mathrm{ms}}\). The proved interval evaluates a policy learned on an independent training half and adds an explicit allowance for its worst-case regret.
technical_internal_limitation: The split-sample construction converts an expected local-polynomial policy-regret bound into coverage by Markov calibration. This produces the exponent \(\rho=s_1(\alpha+1)/(2s_1+d)\), which is strictly smaller than \(\xi\) under the admissibility condition. Thus \(C_n^\dagger\) attains the unified lower scale only in the root-\(n\) subregime \(2s_1\alpha\ge d\). When \(2s_1\alpha<d\), it is unknown whether a fully specified multiscale interval \(C_n^{\mathrm{ms}}\) with sharper high-probability regret or bias control improves the upper rate, or whether a different causal least-favorable experiment strengthens the lower bound.
honest_scope: The sampling model is iid observed-data sampling from a randomized binary-treatment law with known propensity \(1/2\), bounded potential outcomes, a conditional-variance floor, and a design density bounded above and below. The union contains an exact-tie Hölder stratum and a near-tie Hölder stratum satisfying the written margin inequality while allowing an arbitrary tie atom. The conclusions concern connected two-sided intervals for the identified optimal-policy value. They do not cover observational propensity estimation, unknown smoothness or margin parameters beyond this frozen pair, nonconnected confidence sets, or a globally sharp adaptation frontier in the open regimes.
